section.title: Feeding the Five Thousand and Walking on Water
section.sectionRationale: Two great signs are set side by side. Moved with compassion for a crowd like sheep without a shepherd, Jesus feeds five thousand from five loaves and two fish. Then, in the dark of the night, He walks on the sea to His straining disciples, speaks the divine name, and stills the wind. Yet the disciples do not understand, for their hearts are hard. Mark presses the question of Jesus' identity through the shepherd who feeds and the Lord who treads the waves.
section.thesis: Jesus is the true Shepherd of Israel who feeds His people in the wilderness and the Lord who walks upon the sea and says, It is I, do not be afraid; and the right response is not the dull, hard-hearted amazement of the disciples but faith that grasps who He is.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the feeding of the five thousand; the walking on the water; and the healings at Gennesaret.

units.label: The feeding of the five thousand

verses.text: Meanwhile, the apostles gathered around Jesus and brought Him news of all they had done and taught.
And He said to them, “Come with Me privately to a solitary place, and let us rest for a while.” For many people were coming and going, and they did not even have time to eat.
So they went away in a boat by themselves to a solitary place.
But many people saw them leaving and recognized them. They ran together on foot from all the towns and arrived before them.
When Jesus stepped ashore and saw a large crowd, He had {{splanchnizomai|compassion}} on them, because they were like sheep without a {{poimen|shepherd}}. And He began to teach them many things.
By now the hour was already late. So the disciples came to Jesus and said, “This is a desolate place, and the hour is already late.
Dismiss the crowd so they can go to the surrounding countryside and villages and buy themselves something to eat.”
But Jesus told them, “You give them something to eat.” They asked Him, “Should we go out and spend two hundred denarii to give all of them bread to eat?”
“Go and see how many loaves you have,” He told them. And after checking, they said, “Five, and two fish.”
Then Jesus directed them to have the people sit in groups on the green grass.
So they sat down in groups of hundreds and fifties.

Taking the five loaves and the two fish and looking up to heaven, Jesus spoke a {{eulogeo|blessing}} and broke the loaves. Then He gave them to His disciples to set before the people. And He divided the two fish among them all.
They all ate and were satisfied,
and the disciples picked up twelve basketfuls of broken pieces of bread and fish.
And there were five thousand men who had eaten the loaves.
Immediately Jesus made His disciples get into the boat and go on ahead of Him to Bethsaida, while He dismissed the crowd.
After bidding them farewell, He went up on the mountain to pray.
When evening came, the boat was in the middle of the sea, and Jesus was alone on land.
He could see that the disciples were straining to row, because the wind was against them. About the fourth watch of the night, Jesus went out to them, walking on the sea. He intended to pass by them,
but when they saw Him walking on the sea, they cried out, thinking He was a ghost,
for they all saw Him and were terrified. But Jesus spoke up at once: “Take courage! {{egoeimi|It is I}}. Do not be afraid.”
Then He climbed into the boat with them, and the wind died down. And the disciples were utterly astounded,
for they had not understood about the loaves, but their hearts had been hardened.

units.label: The healings at Gennesaret
units.note: all who touched Him were healed

verses.text: When they had crossed over, they landed at Gennesaret and moored the boat.
As soon as they got out of the boat, the people recognized Jesus
and ran through that whole region, carrying the sick on mats to wherever they heard He was.
And wherever He went, villages and towns and countrysides, they laid the sick in the marketplaces and begged Him just to let them touch the fringe of His cloak. And all who touched Him were healed.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion, a mercy felt in the depths. Jesus looks at the leaderless crowd and is stirred to His core, and His compassion moves Him first to teach them and then to feed them, meeting both their deepest and their bodily need.

poimen.gloss: Shepherd. Israel's kings and leaders were meant to shepherd God's people, and God Himself promised to be their Shepherd. Jesus sees the crowd as sheep without a shepherd and steps into the role, the true Shepherd who feeds His flock in the wilderness.

eulogeo.gloss: To bless, to speak well of, to give thanks. Jesus looks to heaven and blesses the loaves before breaking them, the ordinary Jewish blessing over bread, here the prelude to a miracle. The same fourfold pattern, took, blessed, broke, gave, reappears at the Last Supper.

egoeimi.gloss: I am, or it is I. The words that reassure the terrified disciples echo the divine name God revealed to Moses, I am. Spoken by the One walking on the sea, they are more than self-identification; they hint that the Lord of the exodus is present in the boat.

basic.context: The apostles return from their mission, and Jesus takes them away to rest, but the crowds get there first. Rather than send them off, Jesus has compassion on them as sheep without a shepherd, teaches them, and then feeds more than five thousand people from five loaves and two fish, with twelve baskets left over. That night He sends the disciples across the lake, and when they are straining against the wind, He comes to them walking on the water, saying, Take courage, it is I, do not be afraid. He gets in the boat and the wind stops. But the disciples are astounded and uncomprehending, because their hearts were hard. Back on shore, crowds bring the sick to touch even the edge of His cloak, and all are healed.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the two signs together. The feeding shows Jesus as the true Shepherd and the giver of bread in the wilderness, evoking Moses and the manna and the shepherd-king. The sea-walking shows Him as the Lord who alone treads the waves, and His words, it is I, echo God's own name. Mark highlights the disciples' failure to understand, warning us that seeing miracles is not the same as grasping who Jesus is.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is moved with splanchnizomai, gut-deep compassion, for the crowd like sheep without a poimen, a shepherd. He eulogeo, blesses, the loaves and multiplies them. On the sea He says egoeimi, I am, or it is I, the words of the divine name, calming their fear.
basic.typology: The feeding in the wilderness recalls the manna God gave through Moses, marking Jesus as the greater Moses and the bread of life. The shepherd feeding the flock fulfils God's promise in Ezekiel to shepherd His scattered people Himself. Walking on the sea shows Jesus doing what the LORD does in the Psalms and Job.
basic.christ: Jesus is the compassionate Shepherd, the provider of bread, and the Lord who walks on the water and bears the divine name. The fourfold action over the loaves, took, blessed, broke, gave, points ahead to the Lord's Supper and to His body given for many.
basic.god: God shepherds and feeds His people, meeting them in desolate places. The One walking on the sea speaks with God's own I am, revealing that in Jesus God Himself has drawn near.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 27 and Ezekiel 34 lament sheep without a shepherd and promise God as Shepherd. Psalm 77 and Job 9 describe God treading the sea. Exodus 3 gives the divine I am.
basic.oneStory: God had promised to shepherd His scattered people Himself and to feed them, and to be the Lord who rules the sea. In Jesus these promises walk the shores of Galilee. He feeds the hungry flock in the wilderness and comes to His people across the deep with the words, it is I, do not be afraid, previews of the greater feast and the final calm He secures through the cross.

context.literary: The feeding of the five thousand is the only miracle in all four Gospels, marking its importance. Mark pairs it with the sea-walking, as he will pair a second feeding, of four thousand, with another boat scene, building toward the disciples' confession and their persistent dullness. Verse 52 explicitly ties the two: they did not understand about the loaves, and their hearts were hard.
context.historical: The green grass, verse 39, fits the spring near Passover. Two hundred denarii was roughly eight months' wages, far beyond the disciples' means. The fourth watch of the night was between three and six in the morning, the darkest, most exhausting stretch for men rowing against a headwind on the notoriously squally lake.
context.canonical: Sheep without a shepherd echoes Moses' prayer, Numbers 27:17, and Ezekiel 34, where God pledges to shepherd His flock Himself. The wilderness feeding recalls the manna of Exodus 16, and the took, blessed, broke, gave pattern anticipates Mark 14:22. Walking on the sea and the I am recall Job 9:8, Psalm 77:19, and Exodus 3:14, casting Jesus in the role of Israel's God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus as the true Shepherd who feeds His people and as the Lord who walks upon the sea bearing the divine name, and to expose the disciples' hardness of heart, urging the reader toward the understanding faith that mere spectacle does not produce.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe unique acts that reveal Jesus' identity; they are not a promise that provisions will always be multiplied or storms always calmed on demand. What is prescriptive is the summons to understand who Jesus is and to trust rather than fear, and the model of His compassion for the crowds.

debates.question: What does it mean that Jesus intended to pass by them, verse 48?

views.label: A deliberate self-revelation
views.case: Passing by echoes how God passed by Moses and Elijah to reveal His glory, Exodus 33:22 and 1 Kings 19:11; Jesus means to disclose Himself as the Lord who treads the sea, not to bypass them uncaring.

views.label: A test of their recognition that becomes rescue
views.case: He would have gone on had they not cried out; His passing near draws out their fear and then their need, and He answers by getting into the boat and stilling the wind.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that the passing by is a theophany, a showing of divine glory, not indifference; Jesus reveals Himself as the Lord and then rescues His frightened people.

debates.question: Why does Mark stress that the disciples did not understand about the loaves?

views.label: A warning that seeing signs is not believing
views.case: Having just witnessed the feeding, they should have known that the One who provides bread also rules the sea; their terror exposes hearts not yet grasping His identity, a caution to all who see much yet trust little.

views.label: A realistic, hopeful portrait of slow disciples
views.case: Mark's honesty about their hardness assures struggling readers that Jesus patiently persists with dull followers; understanding will come, but only through His continued teaching and, ultimately, the cross and resurrection.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the miracles were meant to teach the disciples who Jesus is, and that hardness of heart, not lack of evidence, is what blinds; the remedy is Christ's patient revelation.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus is moved with splanchnizomai, a compassion felt in the depths, when He sees the crowd as sheep without a poimen, a shepherd, the very image of leaderless Israel in Moses and Ezekiel. Taking the loaves, He eulogeo, blesses and gives thanks, in the fourfold action that will reappear at the Last Supper. On the water He speaks the reassurance egoeimi, I am, or it is I, the words that carry the weight of God's own name revealed at the burning bush. The vocabulary quietly answers the disciples' earlier question, who is this: He is the Shepherd who feeds His flock and the Lord who walks upon the sea.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus the true and greater Moses and the bread of life, giving the bread the manna foreshadowed, Exodus 16 and John 6:35.

typology.type: The Shepherd of the scattered flock
typology.fulfillment: Jesus fulfilling God's promise to shepherd His people Himself, Ezekiel 34:11-16 and John 10:11.

typology.type: The Lord who treads the sea and says I am
typology.fulfillment: Jesus doing what God alone does and bearing the divine name, Job 9:8, Psalm 77:19, and Exodus 3:14.

study.christ: Jesus is unveiled as the Shepherd-King of Israel, moved with compassion for the leaderless flock, teaching and feeding them in the wilderness as God promised to do. The way He takes, blesses, breaks, and gives the bread foreshadows the night He will take bread and say, this is My body, given for many. And when He walks upon the storm-tossed sea and speaks the words it is I, do not be afraid, He does what the Scriptures ascribe to God alone and speaks the divine name to His terrified people. The One in the boat is the Lord of the exodus, present to shepherd, feed, and save.
study.god: God had promised to gather His scattered sheep and shepherd them Himself, to feed them, and to rule the raging sea. In Jesus these divine works are done before the disciples' eyes. God's compassion moves Him to teach and to satisfy the hungry, and His sovereign presence comes across the deep with the reassurance of His own name. The passage insists that in Jesus we meet not merely a wonder-worker but the God who shepherds, provides, and stills the sea, and it calls hard hearts to understand and trust Him.

crossReferences.note: God's promise to seek out His scattered sheep and shepherd them Himself, fulfilled as Jesus feeds the leaderless crowd.
The LORD feeding Israel with bread in the wilderness, the pattern behind the feeding of the five thousand.
Your path led through the sea, Your way through the mighty waters, ascribing to God the act Jesus performs.
He took bread, blessed and broke it, and gave it to them, the same fourfold action that points to His body given.

study.oneStory: God pledged to shepherd His scattered people, to feed them in the wilderness, and to rule the sea that symbolized chaos and death. In Jesus these promises take flesh on the shores of Galilee. He gathers the leaderless crowd, feeds them to the full with bread to spare, and then comes to His straining disciples across the deep with the words of God's own name, it is I, do not be afraid. These signs point beyond themselves to the greater feast He will host and the final calm He will win at the cross, when the Shepherd lays down His life for the sheep and rises to bring them home.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus have compassion on the crowd before feeding them?

options.text: Because they were like sheep without a shepherd, so He taught them and then fed them.
options.note: Mark 6:34; His compassion for the leaderless flock moved Him to teach and provide.

options.text: Because they were hungry and had travelled far.
options.note: Their hunger was real, but Mark grounds His compassion in their being leaderless sheep, met first with teaching.

options.text: Because they had earned His help by following Him.
options.note: His compassion was freely given, not a reward for their pursuit.

options.text: Because the disciples urged Him to feel sorry for them.
options.note: The disciples wanted to send them away; the compassion was Jesus' own.

quiz.q: What does the feeding of the five thousand reveal about Jesus?

options.text: He is the true Shepherd and greater Moses who feeds His people in the wilderness, with abundance to spare.
options.note: The wilderness feeding and twelve baskets left over evoke the manna and mark Jesus as Shepherd and provider.

options.text: He is able to meet people's physical needs.
options.note: True, but the sign points deeper, to His identity as the shepherd-provider foreshadowed in Moses and Ezekiel.

options.text: He wanted to impress the crowd with a spectacle.
options.note: The miracle flowed from compassion and revealed His identity, not a desire to impress.

options.text: He needed the disciples' five loaves to have enough.
options.note: He multiplied the little that was there; the abundance came from Him, not from sufficient supplies.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Jesus' words on the water, take courage, it is I?

options.text: It is I echoes God's own name, I am, revealing the One walking on the sea as the Lord present with His people.
options.note: The phrase recalls Exodus 3:14 and the God who treads the sea, more than mere self-identification.

options.text: He was simply telling them it was Him, not a ghost.
options.note: He did identify Himself, but the words carry the deeper weight of the divine name and God's sea-treading power.

options.text: He was showing off a miraculous ability.
options.note: The act reveals His divine identity and comes to rescue them, not to show off.

options.text: He meant that the disciples should have rowed harder.
options.note: His words comforted their fear and revealed who He is; they were not a rebuke about rowing.

quiz.q: Why were the disciples astounded and uncomprehending after Jesus stilled the wind?

options.text: They had not understood about the loaves, because their hearts had been hardened.
options.note: Mark 6:52 names the cause: hardness of heart that failed to grasp who Jesus is.

options.text: They were simply tired from rowing all night.
options.note: They were exhausted, but Mark specifies the deeper problem was their hard, uncomprehending hearts.

options.text: They had never seen Jesus do anything remarkable before.
options.note: They had just seen the feeding and much else; the issue was failing to understand it, not lack of evidence.

options.text: They correctly understood everything about Jesus.
options.note: The opposite; Mark says they did not understand and their hearts were hard.

quiz.q: What does Mark's honesty about the disciples' hard hearts teach us?

options.text: That witnessing miracles is not the same as grasping who Jesus is; understanding comes through His patient revelation.
options.note: Seeing signs did not cure their dullness; only Christ's continued teaching and the cross would.

options.text: That the disciples were unusually slow compared to others.
options.note: They were slow, but the lesson is broader: hardness of heart, not lack of proof, blinds anyone.

options.text: That miracles are unnecessary for faith.
options.note: The miracles were meant to teach them; the point is that signs alone do not soften a hard heart.

options.text: That Jesus gives up on those who fail to understand.
options.note: Jesus patiently persists with His dull disciples rather than abandoning them.

questions: Jesus saw the crowd as sheep without a shepherd and was moved to teach and feed them. Where do you most need the Good Shepherd's guidance and provision right now?
Jesus told the disciples, you give them something to eat, when they had almost nothing. Where is God asking you to offer your little and trust Him to multiply it?
In the darkest watch of the night, Jesus came to His straining disciples across the sea. How does it encourage you that He comes to His people in their hardest, most exhausting moments?
The disciples saw the feeding yet did not understand who Jesus was. Are there ways you have seen God work and still failed to trust Him as you should?
Jesus said, take courage, it is I, do not be afraid. What fear would loosen its grip if you truly believed the Lord Himself was present with you in it?

translationNotes.note: The disciples' answer, Five, and two fish, originally used a dash for the pause; rendered here with a comma without changing the meaning.

He intended to pass by them uses language that elsewhere describes God passing by to reveal His glory, Exodus 33:22 and 1 Kings 19:11, suggesting a deliberate self-revelation rather than indifference.
It is I translates the Greek ego eimi, I am, the words of the divine name in Exodus 3:14; on the lips of the One walking the sea they hint at Jesus' divine identity.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 6:30-56. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. The em dashes in verses 38, 49, and 56 were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
